metric_value: 'Sun can use AMD's Opteron to compete directly against IBM in the head-to-head Opteron battle for price-conscious buyers of high-performance computers' — Peter Kastner, The Aberdeen Group
Kastner: the Opteron move diversifies product offerings and could help retain customers. 'Without those customers, Sun doesn't stand much of a chance.'
notes: Customer-retention thesis — ultimately did not save Sun

metric_name: Sun announces Opteron-based Sun Fire servers
metric_value: Sun announced at Comdex 2003 it would build Sun Fire servers using AMD Opteron 64-bit processors, with early-access units shipping to developers in 2003 and volume in 2004
notes: Actually launched as Sun Fire x64 line 2004-2005

metric_value: Sun named preferred technology partner for China Standard Software to develop Chinese Linux desktops using Java Desktop System as foundation, at $100/user; initial 500K-1M units in 2004 with target of 200M copies
notes: Ambitious target never materialized at scale

metric_value: 'It's a lot easier to sell to a whole country vis-a-vis a government than it is to go company by company and house by house. It's a very interesting way to approach a market.' — Dana Gardner, Yankee Group
Sun reported net loss of $286M for quarter ended Sep 28 2003 (more than doubled YoY); revenue declining >2 years; stock closed at $4.08 on below-average volume

metric_name: Sun acquired by Oracle, Opteron strategy ended
metric_value: Oracle completed Sun acquisition January 2010 for $7.4B; Oracle deprioritized Sun x64 server business in favor of SPARC/Exadata engineered systems; x64 Sun Fire line wound down by 2013
notes: Kastner's customer-retention bet did not save Sun

metric_value: Sun Java Desktop System rollout to China never approached 200M target; Sun discontinued Java Desktop development ~2006; Chinese government subsequently backed Red Flag Linux and later Kylin/UOS
notes: Gardner country-level framing intriguing but specific deal failed
